author: @HalEvans
text:
Robert, fabulous as always.  I LOVE your channel and in particular your explorations for generating electricity. 
However, I would love to see you address the practicalities of how we all could start  using that electricity sustainably. Fine if one is off grid with space and budget, but that's not the case for most.  As an example, do you sustainably power any aspect of your workshop/lab/experiments/home independent of a utility provided supply using your demo technologies?

A turbine (assisted) charged  electric car may be a conduit to practically explore this (at a home/individual level) with the car's electrical storage being made available for use in the home, not just for driving. A car sitting for 90% of its life but being used as a power wall, charged (in part) by wind really can provide a return of investment.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
I think theres a number of places on a car you could stick a wind turbine where its a brick Anyway, that could reclaim some of the power used drive it- particularly as the wind resistance doesnt care if the the turbine blades are spinning or not, as I understand it. 
I think a squirrel cage off the back of the car where theres that vorex causing drag Anyway, would be a prime spot to put one without additional losses. 

Or a rose turbine or similar  nose cone. You have to pay to push that air out of the way Anyway. Might as well borrow some of that back- to my mind anyway.

author: @frankjohannessen6383
text: Electric cars generally come with regenerative breaking, so in theory you should be able to  jack up your car, attach a rotor to one of the wheels that are attached to the drive-train, put the car in drive and apply the brakes. Or in other words, use the electric motor of the car as the generator of the wind turbine. You would probably need gearing to, since the wheels would probably need to rotate at higher rpm than the rpm of the rotor.

text: Hmm. Just use alcohol in all the ICE engines.. let's go back 100 years and forget Rockefeller ever took over ICE engine fuels.. author: @coachgeo
text:
the white car with the 4? 5? turbines on top was interesting. Came across it years ago.  He designed it to capitalize more on turbulent air if I understood it right.     His design has made me wonder if using vehicles existing turbulent air to drive a turbine.... could smooth out  turbulence thusly reducing drag..... from "the turbulence"..  but gain electric power from the turbine.  net effect once turbine is used to make power....... might in end result in same drag with turbine in the mix ... butttttt...  you got electric power out of it along the way. Where with previous turbulence NOT being capitalized by turbine... you got nothing out if it but drag.  Guess then it matters if electric power produced via turbine powered by smoothing out turbulent air..  is enough or of a usable manner to be a benefit when compared to just reducing the turbulence only and allowing vehicle's engine to work less.... saving fuel.   

Was thinking more of big rig truck or camper where turbulence created is huge and you can't do much about that when it comes to dollar in.. value out.    For example on a big truck/camper; use turbine to reduce turbulence; thus  from that you get less drag... yet...while doing that, turbine  produced electricity within that mess........ yes the turbine itself may in the end bring total drag back to where it was without the turbine... butt there was electricity the turbine created  that could be used to say- "produce HHO".  aka Using turbine's electrical output; eliminating need for alternator power,  to produce the HHO* .  HHO then mighhhht  actually produce some  MPG improvement.*

* alternator use typically makes HHO worthless in mpg value cause of added work on the engine that drives the alternator to produce the electrical input for HHO production.   Granted even with that no real MPG gain..... it may help in very long term just via the added HHO should help in keeping clean engine due to the improved combustion.

replies.author: @rationalmaximiser2
replies.text: Yeah this is huge. Graphene reinforcement for regular cement is nice to have - with a potential weight saving of 40%. But it is much more than that for aircrete. Graphene is the reinforcement material at the micro scale that aircrete has been missing, with aircrete being 'crumbly' in its normal state due to the vastly reduced solid volume compared to regular concrete. Graphene can solve the crumbling problem of aircrete.  And it can be made at home in a blender. I can't wait to see when aircrete harry, and all the other aircrete guys discover this.

author: @kadmow
text:
Yes, things can, and sometimes should be done for fun.  In general, if a vehicle has such bad aero that extracting energy from the windstream improves its performance, there are a lot of energy gains left on the table (for improved design). For energy recovery in the parked setting an energy sculpture is fun, and may charge the batteries etc... (A self furling downwind  HAWT is a candidate, being easy to furl and stow for minimal drag - on the go.

Remember even trees growing by the roadside affects the energy used by passing vehicles, adding weight and aero drag isn't a zero sum game, fun can be had ...

Demonstrating to folk that toy car and its performance at all points of "sail":would be edumicational - ie. speeds; dead-upwind and dead-downwind, as well as direct crosswind...

author: @ChristopherSchreib-yn1vu
text: Dear Sir, why does a wind power device need blades? What you make a kind of vertical air billows, that look like a wood screw on end, with the spiraling ribs tapering upward? By mounting that thing on top of an electric car, then , it could be retracted bu pumping air out it into a small package on the roof, and when the electric car is parked, be inflated to extend it into a tapered tower. Then, when the wind hits it, this vertical corkscrew-shaped billows, will bend and flex, and so compress a volume of compressed air inside it, through an internal spigot in the base, that jets into a smaller, accordion-piston, that is under constant pressure by a steel spring containing that part, within a bigger cylinder that lets it push and pull a rod on its end, to empower a push-me-pull-you gearbox, that turns an electric generator to recharge the electric car’s batteries, while it’s parked. It could also have an aerodynamically-flattened dome on it’s top, with solar cells on it, for additional electric juice, which would also act as an air foil, to catch more wind energy, with a safety air-valve, that automatically deflates this vertical billows, when the winds are too strong, and even a shorter extension of it would provide some electrical power, during a tornado season. And a steel cable reinforced coil in its construction, could insure it doesn’t do harm, like a flying windmill broken blade snapped off in tornado force winds can, no blade shrapnel! And the steel cable coil within its skin-envelope could also serve as a radio antenna. Finally, it might be possible to manufacture this ‘ScrewTop” electric car rooftop windmill, out of repurposed materials, like discarded fireman hoses, strips from trashed automobile tires, and miscellaneous plastic waste, which should have the high tensile strength and durability for this!

replies.author: @BucurStefan
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Sorry for the delay, I was thinking of an array of horizontal axys savonius rotors mounted on top of eachother making it look like a fence. This fence is mounted parallel with the direction of travel and in the middle on the top of the car making also the rotor axys be parralel with the direction of travel. The rotor will not be influenced by the speed of the car and will be only activated by the sidewinds. This setup is made  that the rotors will work for the sidewinds and minimise the drag created. The rotor diameter will be relative small, around 20cm, but the array of them will create several square meters of wind turbine surface.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Honestly, I don't get it. Blowing the wind turbine from behind isn't the same as driving the car "INTO" the wind. So, while these gimmicks would work when the car wasn't moving (parked car), when the car is actually moving they will just "drag" the overall efficiency down. So, yes they will make energy, but they will use more energy than the car engine alone when driving.
You can't rely on the wind to be a certain direction relative to the car either.

author: @KavanOBrien

author: @wodgesad
text: I’ve always thought you could use wind whilst driving instead of the alternator and maybe power a hho generator to create hydrogen to help the engine. I think a turbine could add less drag the the alternator on the engine and have the bonus of keeping the battery topped off whilst parked. Even more useful on a motorhome which is already brick shaped so nearly impossible to make less efficient by putting a turbine on the front unless it increases frontal area you can also put it up high above pedestrians

replies.author: @Dirt-Diggler
replies.text: My main experience with wind turbines is removing them and adding solar panels to narrow boats.

replies.author: @wodgesad
replies.text: @Dirt-Diggler I agree but unlike canal boats cars move fast enough to guarantee wind whilst driving

author: @russellzauner
text:
I've often wondered if a generator that takes less energy to move than the vehicle is pushing could be used to maintain locomotion - it's not free energy, it's just smart harvesting.  Much in the way we've discovered that fully passive cooling is 100% legit if you partition the problem and apply the right materials in the right way, I think if we have a couple/few technologies running in cooperation we can increase efficiencies as well as harvest energy from multiple methods while under locomotion, the sum of which could possibly be enough to maintain or even begin continuous motion.
As long as the technologies don't borrow much from your momentum or other stored energies (or don't borrow from there at all) we could roll around all day long once the right configuration is discovered.

replies.author: @Dirt-Diggler
replies.text: It takes roughly 35 BHP to keep a modern car in motion on a motorway, way less than it takes to accelerate the same vehicle to that speed, that's why fuel economy is way way better on a motorway drive so whatever method you use to keep the vehicle at that speed you'd still need to produce the same power to do it, even IF a generator was more efficient than the car engine you'd have losses changing that power from mechanical to electric and back to mechanical again ??

author: @josephpk4878
text: I always answered this question by telling people that perpetual motion hasn't been invented, yet, and "No" you're not going to power a car with a wind turbine that creates more drag than the electrical energy it produces to overcome net zero. author: @dans-designs
text: great video, ive always thought your flat feather blade rotor would be perfect for use on a car, when standing still it has 360 degree accessibility and when the car is running it will use the airflow going over the car.. the best place for capturing this airflow would be at the back where the spoiler would go

replies.author: @nitcat1
replies.text: Incorporate vertically oriented versions in front of the wheels and let that wind passing through also cool the brakes

replies.author: @dans-designs
replies.text: @nitcat1 that's a brilliant idea! Running cables from the wheels could be interesting 🤔😅

replies.author: @nitcat1
replies.text: @dans-designs these would be separate from the wheel - race cars often have vents to cool brakes. Who says a fender can’t include a vent to a fan that also cools brakes.

replies.author: @billy-go9kx
replies.text: @nitcat1 Why do you need your brakes to be cooled????  Don't use them.  Use regenerative braking and your brakes will last forever.

replies.author: @dans-designs
replies.text: @nitcat1 ahhh i get what you mean.. yea sounds good for while the car is moving!
